column2: A wireless design must both protect traffic from listeners and verify which users may connect. Why are encryption and authentication both needed?
column4: Encryption protects the wireless data, while authentication verifies the user or device requesting access.
column5: These controls solve different problems. WPA encryption does not by itself provide individual identity verification, and an authentication server does not replace wireless traffic encryption.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.3; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 30
column10: A+::220-1202::2.3 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WirelessSecurityProtocolsandAuthentication Basic HighYield WirelessSecurity Encryption Authentication Source::Messer-v140

column2: How do the encryption methods commonly associated with WPA2 and the original WPA differ?
column4: WPA2 uses Advanced Encryption Standard (AES), while the original WPA used the transitional Temporal Key Integrity Protocol (TKIP).
column5: AES is stronger than TKIP. This card tests the protocol-to-encryption association; 2.3-B005 separately tests which method to select when both are offered.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.3; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 31
column10: A+::220-1202::2.3 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WirelessSecurityProtocolsandAuthentication Basic HighYield WPA2 AES WirelessEncryption Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which two security improvements can justify upgrading a compatible wireless network from WPA2 to WPA3?
column4: WPA3 offers stronger cryptographic options and better protection for the initial key exchange.
column5: WPA3 also supports encryption on open networks, which 2.3-B004 tests separately.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.3; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 31
column10: A+::220-1202::2.3 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WirelessSecurityProtocolsandAuthentication Basic HighYield WPA2 WPA3 WirelessEncryption Source::Messer-v140

column2: A public wireless network does not require an authentication password but should still encrypt each user's wireless traffic. Which Objective 2.3 protocol adds that capability?
column5: WPA3 can protect traffic on an open network. “Open” describes the lack of a shared authentication password; it does not require the radio traffic to be sent without encryption.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.3; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 31
column10: A+::220-1202::2.3 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WirelessSecurityProtocolsandAuthentication Basic WPA3 OpenNetworks WirelessEncryption Source::Messer-v140

column2: When compatible hardware offers AES or TKIP for wireless encryption, which should be selected for stronger protection?
column5: TKIP let the original WPA improve security on older hardware and served as a bridge to WPA2. AES is the stronger encryption choice associated with WPA2.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.3; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 31
column10: A+::220-1202::2.3 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WirelessSecurityProtocolsandAuthentication Basic HighYield TKIP AES WirelessEncryption Source::Messer-v140

column2: An organization wants each wireless user authenticated individually by a central server instead of giving everyone the same pre-shared key. Should it use WPA Personal or WPA Enterprise?
column4: WPA Enterprise with 802.1X and an authentication server such as RADIUS.
column5: Personal mode uses a shared network key. Enterprise mode delegates individual authentication to a central service while the WPA protocol protects the wireless traffic.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.3; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 31
column10: A+::220-1202::2.3 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WirelessSecurityProtocolsandAuthentication Basic HighYield WPAEnterprise WPAPersonal RADIUS PSK Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which authentication protocol can let wireless 802.1X, VPN, router, or switch services validate users through a central authentication server?
column5: RADIUS is broadly supported for centralized AAA. Despite “dial-in” in its name, its use is not limited to dial-up access.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.3; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 31
column10: A+::220-1202::2.3 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WirelessSecurityProtocolsandAuthentication Basic HighYield RADIUS AAA CentralizedAuthentication Source::Messer-v140

column2: A network-device environment, particularly one built around Cisco equipment, needs a compatible remote authentication protocol. Which Objective 2.3 method is the likely fit?
column5: Protocol selection depends on what the devices and authentication service support. TACACS+ is commonly associated with Cisco equipment; it is an authentication method, not wireless encryption.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.3; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, pp. 31–32
column10: A+::220-1202::2.3 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WirelessSecurityProtocolsandAuthentication Basic HighYield TACACSPlus NetworkDevices Authentication Source::Messer-v140

column2: A Microsoft-oriented network needs ticket-based single sign-on and mutual authentication between clients and services. Which authentication protocol fits?
column5: Kerberos uses cryptographic tickets after the initial authentication, reducing repeated credential entry and helping resist replay and on-path attacks.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.3; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 31
column10: A+::220-1202::2.3 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WirelessSecurityProtocolsandAuthentication Basic HighYield Kerberos SSO MutualAuthentication Source::Messer-v140

column2: A sign-in requires a password followed by a code from a hardware token. Why is this multifactor authentication, while requiring two passwords is not?
column4: The password and token come from different factor categories—something known and something possessed. Two passwords are both knowledge factors.
column5: MFA counts distinct factor categories, not merely the number of prompts. It adds authentication assurance but does not provide wireless encryption.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.3; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 32
